teachings.teaching: Even a sinful person who hears this hymn in Shiva's presence attains Brahmaloka.
teachings.lens_note: This underscores the grace of Shiva and the efficacy of satsang (holy company) and listening to sacred texts, which Sharma would see as a form of Kriya-Yoga's purification.

chapter_summary: Shiva grants Brahma devotion and then departs. Brahma, attempting to create, becomes frustrated and sheds tears that become serpents. In anger, he faints and dies, but from his body arise eleven Rudras. Shiva revives Brahma, who then sees the universe in Shiva and praises him. Shiva explains the Śvetakalpa and the Sadyojāta mantra.

story.title: Shiva's Grace and Brahma's Creation of Rudras
story.arc: Shiva blesses Brahma, who then creates serpents from tears, dies, and is revived; the origin of Rudras and the revelation of the Śvetakalpa and Sadyojāta mantra.
story.timeline_note: Occurs during the Śvetakalpa, a cosmic era.
story.comic_potential: Brahma's dramatic death and revival, the birth of Rudras from his body, and the cosmic revelations.

teachings.teaching: Devotion to Shiva leads to liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, devotion (bhakti) is a key practice that aligns the practitioner's will with the divine, leading to inner transformation and ultimately liberation.

teachings.teaching: The universe is a manifestation of Shiva.
teachings.lens_note: This echoes the Kriya-Yoga view that the entire cosmos is a projection of the supreme consciousness, and realizing this unity is a goal of meditation.

teachings.teaching: The Sadyojāta mantra is a secret that grants liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, mantras are tools for focusing the mind and invoking divine energies. The Sadyojāta mantra, associated with Shiva's immediate manifestation, is used to transcend rebirth.

chapter_summary: Rudra explains to the sage his manifestations in different yugas as various forms (Lohita, Vamadeva, Tatpurusha, Ghora, Vishvarupa) corresponding to the colors of the Gayatri, and how those who know these forms attain liberation.

story.title: Rudra's Manifestations in Different Yugas
story.arc: Rudra reveals his forms corresponding to the colors of the Gayatri and the yugas, and the fruits of knowing them.
story.timeline_note: The narrative spans multiple yugas (Lohita, Pita, Krishna, Vishvarupa) as described by Rudra.
story.comic_potential: A cosmic montage of Rudra changing colors and forms, with Gayatri matching each hue, culminating in a multi-armed Vishvarupa.

teachings.teaching: Those who know Rudra's forms (Lohita, Vamadeva, etc.) attain Rudraloka and liberation from rebirth.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the practitioner realizing the divine in each stage of practice, transcending the gunas.

teachings.teaching: The Gayatri is the mother of the Vedas and manifests in different colors corresponding to Rudra's forms.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as the unity of sound (Gayatri) and form (Rudra) in meditation.

teachings.teaching: The four sons (putrakah) represent the four purusharthas, four Vedas, four ashramas, etc.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, this symbolizes the integration of all aspects of life into spiritual practice.

chapter_summary: Rudra explains to Brahma the cosmic significance of the Gayatri mantra, describing how the four-footed and two-footed beings arise from its forms, and how the seven worlds correspond to its feet. He reveals that those who meditate on him through Gayatri attain liberation.

story.title: Rudra's Discourse on Gayatri and the Cosmic Order
story.arc: Brahma questions Rudra about the forms of the goddess and the nature of the worlds; Rudra reveals the esoteric meaning of Gayatri and the path to liberation.
story.timeline_note: Occurs in a primordial setting before creation of beings.
story.comic_potential: A dialogue between two supreme deities explaining cosmic geometry and mantra mysticism.

teachings.teaching: The Gayatri mantra has four feet corresponding to the four worlds (bhur, bhuva, svar, maha) and leads to Vishnu's realm, which is beyond return.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this correlates to the four stages of pranayama and the ascent of kundalini through the chakras to the crown.

teachings.teaching: Those free from ego, desire, and anger, and absorbed in meditation, will see Rudra's supreme form and attain his abode.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that Kriya-Yoga purifies the mind of rajas and tamas, enabling direct perception of the divine.

teachings.teaching: The four-footed beings (animals) and two-footed beings (humans) arise from the forms of Gayatri, and the sacred fire (Agni) is present in all creatures.
teachings.lens_note: This illustrates the immanence of the divine in all life, a key principle in Sharma's non-dual Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: Shiva (Mahadeva) reveals to Brahma that he cannot be seen by humans through tapas, rituals, or study, but only through meditation (dhyana). He then prophesies his future incarnations in each Dvapara Yuga of the Vaivasvata Manvantara, along with his disciples, who will attain Rudraloka through yoga.

story.title: Shiva's Prophecy of Incarnations
story.arc: Shiva explains the necessity of meditation for divine vision and foretells his future births as sages in each Dvapara Yuga, along with their disciples who will attain liberation.
story.timeline_note: Set in the Vaivasvata Manvantara, spanning multiple Dvapara Yugas.
story.comic_potential: The repetitive structure of incarnations and disciples could be humorously depicted as a cosmic cycle of teaching and liberation.

teachings.teaching: Shiva cannot be seen through tapas, rituals, donations, pilgrimages, sacrifices, Vedic study, or wealth, but only through meditation (dhyana).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes that inner meditative practice (kriya) is the direct path to realizing the divine, surpassing external observances.

teachings.teaching: Through yoga and meditation, one can attain Rudraloka, a state of liberation with no return.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret this as the fruit of sustained kriya-yoga practice, leading to union with Shiva (Rudra) and freedom from rebirth.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of Vyasa in each Dvapara Yuga and the corresponding incarnations of the speaker (a sage) as a disciple or teacher, along with their disciples. It lists the names of Vyasa, the speaker's alias, and the disciples who attain Rudraloka through yoga.

story.title: The Incarnations of Vyasa and the Speaker in Each Dvapara
story.arc: Prophetic listing of future births and disciples across multiple Dvapara Yugas.
story.timeline_note: Spans from the fifth Dvapara to the eleventh Dvapara, with each cycle featuring a different incarnation of Vyasa and the speaker.
story.comic_potential: A cosmic roll-call of sages and their disciples across ages, with repetitive structure that could be visualized as a montage.

teachings.teaching: Through yoga and meditation, one can attain Rudraloka, a realm free from return.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the transformative power of disciplined yogic practice to achieve liberation.

teachings.teaching: The path of dhyāna yoga (meditation) is the means to reach the supreme goal.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga focuses on dhyāna as a core practice; this verse reinforces that meditation is the direct path.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of the sage Vyasa in each of the fourteen (and beyond) Dvapara yugas, along with the speaker's own incarnations as a disciple of Vyasa and the names of his sons who attain Rudraloka through Maheshvara yoga.

story.title: The Successive Incarnations of Vyasa and the Speaker
story.arc: The chapter lists the names of Vyasa and the speaker in each Dvapara yuga, and the names of the speaker's sons who, through Maheshvara yoga, attain Rudraloka.
story.timeline_note: The chapter covers fourteen (and more) Dvapara yugas, from the eleventh to the seventeenth and beyond, in a cyclic pattern.
story.comic_potential: The repetitive structure of listing names and the same outcome for each yuga could be depicted as a cosmic cycle with recurring characters in different forms.

teachings.teaching: Through the practice of Maheshvara yoga, one can attain Rudraloka, the abode of Rudra.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this emphasizes the transformative power of disciplined yogic practice (kriya) to achieve liberation and union with the divine, here personified as Rudra.

teachings.teaching: The sage Vyasa incarnates in every Dvapara yuga to propagate dharma and yoga.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's commentary would see this as the eternal recurrence of the guru principle, guiding humanity through the ages via the same essential teachings of Kriya-Yoga.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of the sage Vyasa and the speaker (a devotee of Shiva) across successive cosmic cycles (parivartas). It lists the names of Vyasa in each cycle, the corresponding name and location of the speaker, and the disciples who attain Rudraloka through the practice of Maheshvara Yoga, emphasizing the importance of meditation on Shiva and the holy site Mahalaya.

story.title: The Successive Incarnations of Vyasa and the Devotee of Shiva
story.arc: Prophetic listing of future cycles (parivartas) where the sage Vyasa and a devotee of Shiva appear in different forms, along with their disciples who attain liberation through Maheshvara Yoga.
story.timeline_note: The chapter covers multiple future cosmic cycles (parivartas) starting from the seventeenth (saptadaśa) up to the twenty-first (ekaviṃśa).
story.comic_potential: The repeated pattern of a new Vyasa and a new devotee of Shiva appearing in each cycle, with disciples following the same path, could be depicted as a series of vignettes showing the continuity of spiritual tradition across ages.

teachings.teaching: By attaining the Maheshvara Yoga (the yoga of the great lord Shiva), one goes to Rudra (Shiva) himself.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this corresponds to the ultimate union with the divine through disciplined practice and devotion, emphasizing that the goal of yoga is not merely liberation but absorption into the supreme consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Meditation on Shiva with the mind fixed in the heart leads to the attainment of Shiva's abode.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga interprets this as the practice of internal meditation (antar dhyana) where the yogi visualizes Shiva in the heart center, leading to the realization of the self as Shiva.

teachings.teaching: Visiting the holy place Mahalaya and seeing the supreme abode of Maheshvara purifies and leads to Rudraloka.
teachings.lens_note: This teaching aligns with the Kriya-Yoga emphasis on pilgrimage (tirtha) as an external aid to internal purification, but the ultimate 'place' is the inner sanctuary of the heart.

teachings.teaching: One who crosses over (the ocean of samsara) helps ten ancestors and ten descendants, and oneself as the twenty-first, by reaching Mahalaya.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga sees this as the power of a realized soul to liberate others through their spiritual attainment, emphasizing the interconnectedness of all beings.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of Vyasa across various parivartas (cycles) and the corresponding births of the speaker (a sage) as different disciples or sons of Vyasa, all devoted to Rudra. It lists the names of Vyasa, the speaker, and their disciples, emphasizing the attainment of Rudraloka through Maheshvara yoga.

story.title: The Future Incarnations of Vyasa and His Disciples
story.arc: Prophetic enumeration of successive parivartas (cycles) where Vyasa appears in different forms, and the speaker (a sage) appears as his disciple or son, all practicing Maheshvara yoga to attain Rudraloka.
story.timeline_note: The chapter covers multiple future parivartas (cycles) from the 21st to the 27th, each with a different Vyasa incarnation.
story.comic_potential: A repetitive listing of names and cycles; could be visualized as a cosmic timeline with recurring patterns of discipleship and liberation.

teachings.teaching: By practicing Maheshvara yoga (the yoga of the great lord Shiva), one attains Rudraloka and immortality.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga commentary, this teaching emphasizes the importance of disciplined yogic practice (kriya) as a means to realize the supreme consciousness (Shiva) and achieve liberation. The repeated mention of 'prāpya māheśvaraṃ yogaṃ' underscores that consistent, devoted practice is the key to transcendence.

teachings.teaching: The path of naiṣṭhika vrata (steadfast vow) and celibacy (ūrdhvaretasa) leads to Rudraloka.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga would interpret this as the necessity of brahmacharya (celibacy) and unwavering commitment to the spiritual path, which purifies the mind and channels energy upward for higher realization.

chapter_summary: In this chapter from the Linga Purana, Lord Shiva describes his future incarnations and the Pashupata yoga path. He reveals that in the 28th cycle, he will incarnate as Lakulisha, with disciples Kushika, Garga, Mitra, and Kaurushya. The chapter emphasizes the supremacy of the five-syllable mantra (Om Namah Shivaya) and the Pashupata vow for liberation, stating that without it, even Sankhya and Pancharatra paths fail. Brahma questions why Vishnu, who is supreme, worships Shiva's linga; Shiva explains that all deities, including Vishnu and Brahma, worship the linga to attain their positions.

story.title: Shiva's Avatars and the Pashupata Yoga
story.arc: Shiva reveals his future incarnation as Lakulisha and the path of Pashupata yoga, while Brahma questions Vishnu's devotion to Shiva.
story.timeline_note: Set in the 28th cycle (manvantara) of the current kalpa, with reference to future events.
story.comic_potential: The dialogue between Brahma and Shiva, where Brahma is puzzled by Vishnu's worship of Shiva, has dramatic and philosophical tension.

teachings.teaching: The Pashupata yoga, involving the five-syllable mantra (Om Namah Shivaya), is the supreme path to liberation; without it, even Sankhya and Pancharatra paths fail.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga commentary, this emphasizes that the direct practice of mantra and meditation on Shiva's form (linga) is essential for inner transformation, beyond mere intellectual paths.

teachings.teaching: All deities, including Vishnu and Brahma, worship the Shiva linga to attain their positions.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's lens interprets this as the unity of all paths: the ultimate reality (Shiva) is the source, and devotion to the linga symbolizes surrender to the formless.

teachings.teaching: One who practices the Pashupata vow, free from dualities, is like a ripe fruit ready for liberation.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Kriya-Yoga's emphasis on inner purification and the ripening of consciousness through disciplined practice.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the origin and importance of the linga worship, focusing on the bath (snāna) ritual before worshiping Śiva. It recounts how Śiva taught the linga worship to Brahmā, who then passed it on to Nandī, Sanatkumāra, and Vyāsa. The chapter details the three types of baths (vāruṇa, āgneya, mantra) and emphasizes inner purity over external cleansing.

story.title: The Origin and Ritual of Linga Worship
story.arc: Instruction on the bath ritual before linga worship, tracing the teaching lineage from Śiva to Vyāsa.
story.timeline_note: The chapter presents a nested narration: Vyāsa recounts what Sanatkumāra heard from Nandī, who heard from Śiva.
story.comic_potential: The emphasis on inner purity over external ritual could be depicted as a humorous contrast between a pompous bather and a truly pure-hearted devotee.

teachings.teaching: Without linga worship, there is no devotion (niṣṭhā).
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this underscores that external ritual is a necessary support for internal discipline; the linga symbolizes the formless, and worshiping it aligns the practitioner with the cosmic energy.

teachings.teaching: Three types of bath: vāruṇa (water), āgneya (fire/ash), mantra (mental).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would interpret these as progressive stages of purification: physical, energetic, and mental. The mantra bath corresponds to Kriya-Yoga's meditative cleansing of the mind.

teachings.teaching: Inner purity (bhāvaśuddhi) is essential; external bathing alone does not purify if the mind is impure.
teachings.lens_note: This aligns with Sharma's emphasis on the inner state over mere ritual. The 'citta kamala' (lotus of the mind) must be awakened by the light of knowledge.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana (Part 1, Chapter 25-26) provides detailed instructions on the ritual of bathing (snāna) and the subsequent worship of Gāyatrī and the sun, including the five great sacrifices (pañca-mahāyajña). It emphasizes purification, mantra recitation, and proper offerings to deities, sages, and ancestors.

story.title: Ritual Bathing and Worship
story.arc: Instructional
story.timeline_note: Timeless ritual instructions.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely procedural.

teachings.teaching: The ritual bath (snāna) with specific mantras and materials purifies and increases merit.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this external purification mirrors internal cleansing of the mind through disciplined practice (kriya).

teachings.teaching: Worship of Gāyatrī and the sun with mantra repetition and offerings leads to spiritual realization.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma would see this as a kriya-yoga practice where sound (mantra) and visualization align the practitioner with cosmic consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The five great sacrifices (pañca-mahāyajña) are essential duties for a householder.
teachings.lens_note: In Kriya-Yoga, these external acts correspond to inner offerings of ego, thoughts, and actions to the divine.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the five great sacrifices (pañca mahāyajña), with emphasis on the Brahma-yajña (sacrifice to Brahman). It details the procedure for performing Brahma-yajña, including purification rituals, recitation of Vedas, and application of sacred ash (bhasma). The chapter also explains the consequences of neglecting these sacrifices and the importance of performing them for spiritual upliftment.

story.title: The Five Great Sacrifices and the Procedure of Brahma-yajña
story.arc: Instructional discourse on ritual practices
story.timeline_note: No specific timeline; timeless teaching.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a ritual manual with no narrative or dramatic elements.

teachings.teaching: The five great sacrifices (pañca mahāyajña) are: Deva-yajña (offering to gods), Bhūta-yajña (offering to beings), Pitṛ-yajña (offering to ancestors), Mānuṣa-yajña (offering to humans), and Brahma-yajña (study and recitation of Vedas). Brahma-yajña is the highest.
teachings.lens_note: In Shailendra Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, these sacrifices are internalized as actions of the subtle body: offering breath to the divine, etc. Brahma-yajña corresponds to the realization of the Self through meditation on the sacred sound.

teachings.teaching: One who neglects the five sacrifices is reborn as a pig.
teachings.lens_note: Symbolically, this indicates that without inner sacrifice (yajña as self-discipline), one remains in ignorance (tamas), like a pig wallowing in mud.

teachings.teaching: The procedure for Brahma-yajña includes purification with water, application of sacred ash (bhasma) on specific body parts corresponding to the five faces of Śiva (Īśāna, Tatpuruṣa, Aghora, Vāma, Sadyā), and recitation of Vedic mantras.
teachings.lens_note: The application of bhasma symbolizes the burning of karmic impressions. In Kriya-Yoga, this is the inner purification through pranayama and meditation, where the five faces represent five aspects of consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the detailed procedure for the ritual worship of Shiva, including purification, preparation of offerings, and meditation on the deity's form. It emphasizes the use of mantras, especially the Pranava and the five-syllable mantra, and outlines the steps for offering arghya, pādya, and ācamana. The chapter also mentions the worship of attendant deities like Nandin, Skanda, Vināyaka, and the goddess.

story.title: Ritual Worship of Shiva
story.arc: Procedural description of liṅga worship
story.timeline_note: Timeless ritual instruction; no narrative sequence.
story.comic_potential: Low; purely ritualistic with no dramatic events.

teachings.teaching: After bathing, one should enter the worship place, perform three prāṇāyāmas, and meditate on Shiva with five faces, ten arms, and white crystal complexion.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, prāṇāyāma purifies the subtle body, and meditation on Shiva's form aligns the practitioner with the cosmic consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The body should be purified and the mūlamantra (Pranava) should be placed in the body in order.
teachings.lens_note: This corresponds to the Kriya-Yoga practice of nyāsa, where mantras are placed on body parts to energize and sanctify them.

teachings.teaching: The five-syllable mantra 'Namaḥ Śivāya' is to be used in the thread (sūtra) as the subtle form of all mantras.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that the pañcākṣara mantra is the essence of all mantras and its repetition leads to union with Shiva.

teachings.teaching: The worship place should be sprinkled with scented water, and all vessels purified with the Pranava.
teachings.lens_note: This external purification mirrors internal purification; in Kriya-Yoga, the environment is consecrated to create a sacred space for meditation.

teachings.teaching: Offerings of arghya, pādya, and ācamana are prepared with specific ingredients like sandalwood, camphor, and kuśa grass.
teachings.lens_note: These offerings symbolize the devotee's surrender and devotion; in Kriya-Yoga, each element corresponds to a subtle energy center.

teachings.teaching: After worshiping the attendants (Nandin, Skanda, Vināyaka, Devī), one enters the inner sanctum and offers a handful of flowers to the five heads of Shiva.
teachings.lens_note: This represents the culmination of the ritual, where the devotee merges with the deity; in Kriya-Yoga, it symbolizes the union of individual consciousness with universal consciousness.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the ritual of bathing (snāpana) the Śiva liṅga with various mantras and substances, detailing the preparation of the seat, invocation of deities, and the specific mantras to be used for purification and liberation from sins.

story.title: Ritual Bathing of the Liṅga
story.arc: Procedural description of liṅga worship
story.timeline_note: No narrative timeline; ritual instructions.
story.comic_potential: Low; highly technical ritual manual.

teachings.teaching: The liṅga should be worshipped with flowers, incense, and various offerings, and then bathed with mantras for purification and liberation.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, this ritual bathing symbolizes the purification of the inner self through disciplined action (kriya), aligning the practitioner with the divine will.

teachings.teaching: The seat (āsana) for the liṅga is visualized as a lotus with petals representing siddhis (aṇimā, etc.) and the pericarp representing Soma, with Sūrya and Pāvaka below, and Dharmādi in the intermediate directions, and Ananta at the base.
teachings.lens_note: This elaborate visualization corresponds to the Kriya-Yoga practice of constructing a subtle inner altar, where each element represents a stage of spiritual ascent.

teachings.teaching: The five faces of Śiva (Sadyojāta, Vāmadeva, Aghora, Īśāna) are invoked with specific mantras, and the Rudragāyatrī is used for presence, Aghora for restraint.
teachings.lens_note: The five faces correspond to the five koshas (sheaths) in Kriya-Yoga, and their invocation purifies each layer of the being.

teachings.teaching: Bathing the liṅga with pañcagavya (five cow products), ghee, honey, sugarcane juice, and other pure substances, using the praṇava and various mantras, removes all sins.
teachings.lens_note: The substances represent the five elements; bathing the liṅga is a symbolic act of balancing the elements within the practitioner, leading to inner purity.

teachings.teaching: A list of mantras for bathing includes Pavamāna, Vāmīyaka, Rudra, Nīlarudra, Śrīsūkta, Rajanīsūkta, Camaka, etc., and the Pañcabrahma and Praṇava.
teachings.lens_note: Each mantra is a vibrational key; reciting them in sequence aligns the practitioner's energy with cosmic rhythms, a core Kriya-Yoga technique.

chapter_summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the detailed procedure for worshipping the Shiva Linga, including offerings of sandalwood paste, flowers, incense, lamps, food, water, and other items. It then transitions to internal worship (ābhyantara pūjā), explaining the meditation on the form of Shiva as both niṣkala (formless) and sakala (with form), the concept of Purusha, the 25 tattvas, and the supremacy of Shiva as the 26th principle beyond Prakriti and Purusha. The chapter also discusses the relationship between the macrocosm and microcosm, and the role of time and karma.

story.title: Linga Worship and Internal Meditation
story.arc: Instruction on external and internal worship of Shiva Linga, culminating in the realization of Shiva as the supreme principle beyond the 25 tattvas.
story.timeline_note: Timeless; the chapter presents eternal truths about worship and cosmology.
story.comic_potential: Low; the chapter is a detailed ritual and philosophical exposition.

teachings.teaching: The external worship of the Shiva Linga involves offerings of sandalwood, flowers, incense, lamp, food, water, and other items, accompanied by the Pranava mantra.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya-Yoga, external worship is a means to purify the mind and prepare for internal meditation, aligning with the principle of karma yoga.

teachings.teaching: Internal worship (ābhyantara pūjā) involves meditating on Shiva as both formless (niṣkala) and with form (sakala), seated with Uma (Kāntārdharūḍhadeha).
teachings.lens_note: Sharma emphasizes that internal worship is the essence of Kriya-Yoga, where the practitioner visualizes the deity within and merges with the divine consciousness.

teachings.teaching: The Purusha (the enjoyer) resides in the body (pura), and the Yajamāna (the performer of sacrifice) is the one who worships through the yajña.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma interprets this as the identification of the individual self with the cosmic self, a key step in Kriya-Yoga meditation.

teachings.teaching: The 25 tattvas (principles) from Prakriti to Purusha are enumerated, and Shiva is the 26th principle beyond them, the supreme Lord.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga teaches that by transcending the 25 tattvas through meditation, one realizes the 26th, which is Shiva, the ultimate reality.

teachings.teaching: Shiva creates Hiranyagarbha (Brahma) and is the source of all, including the sun and moon (Bhānu and Śaśin).
teachings.lens_note: This reinforces the concept of Shiva as the supreme creator, which in Kriya-Yoga is realized as the inner light of consciousness.

teachings.teaching: Time (Kāla) does everything, but it is controlled by Shiva, who is niṣkala (without parts) and yet the support of all.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma's Kriya-Yoga views time as a manifestation of Shiva's power; meditation on the timeless self liberates one from time's bondage.

teachings.teaching: The entire universe is the eightfold form (mūrtyaṣṭaka) of Shiva, consisting of space, earth, air, fire, water, and the sacrificer (yajamāna), sun, and moon.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma teaches that perceiving the universe as Shiva's body is a form of cosmic meditation, integrating the microcosm with the macrocosm.
